A researcher is told to identify the scarcest resource in explanation. If they begin with the usual candidates—data, compute, or concise wording—they immediately face the first premise: reframing may be scarcer than any of these, because a reframe does not merely add a better description of fixed things. It alters what is treated as a thing, what counts as a relation, and which features are even eligible to be properties. Under that condition, the main bottleneck is upstream of description. The hard part is not selecting among known objects and relations, but changing the ontology that makes those selections possible.

They then try to rescue a criterion of “minimal assumptions.” But the second premise blocks any stable use of that criterion. If explanations that once seemed assumption-heavy later appeared simple inside a better framework, then assumption-count is not framework-invariant. What looked bloated may really have been compressed structure expressed in the wrong vocabulary. So “minimal assumptions” cannot function as a neutral judge across frameworks; it depends on the very reframing whose scarcity was already identified.

Suppose someone objects with a search-space argument: producing a good explanation should be incredibly unlikely, because a 1,000-word account over a 50,000-word vocabulary yields an astronomical number of possible sequences. But the third premise undercuts the force of that objection. Einstein’s journalized explanation was not generated by independent word draws. Its words were constrained by grammar, prior concepts, mathematical structure, and the explanatory target itself. Therefore the raw combinatorial count does not track the actual generative process. It may remain rhetorically useful for conveying vastness, but it is structurally irrelevant to the real search, which is organized by dependencies and frameworks rather than independent token choice.

At this point the researcher has learned three things. First, explanation is governed by reframing. Second, simplicity cannot be measured independently of framing. Third, brute combinatorics over surface strings mischaracterizes the search. So any account of explanatory difficulty that ignores ontological shifts and structured generation is aimed at the wrong level.

The remaining premises introduce strings that do not transparently integrate with the others: “Descriers forrit fruitful clonus heptyne” and “Paused metropathic brokenhearted concertmeister droops.” Since all premises must be taken as true, these utterances exist as part of the world under description. But given only the provided axioms, no determinate semantics for them follows. Their presence therefore demonstrates the earlier points rather than escaping them. Whether these strings are noise, technical terms, corrupted language, or meaningful within an unknown framework cannot be settled by assumption-minimization alone, nor by counting how improbable they are as word sequences. Only a successful reframe could determine what objects, relations, or properties they express, if any.

So the world implied by the premises is one in which explanatory success depends less on traversing huge lexical possibility spaces and more on finding the framework in which both “simplicity” and even “meaningful objecthood” become visible.
